content.text: Based on the official Claude documentation, Anthropic's "Building Effective AI Agents" research, and the Claude Code Harness pattern documentation,
"Memory & State"
is how agents
maintain continuity across turns and sessions
, enabling them to remember past interactions and preserve context rather than starting from scratch each time.
What It Is
Memory & State encompasses
state, persistence, and knowledge across sessions
— the mechanisms that allow agents to remember what happened in previous turns, retain knowledge from past interactions, and maintain context across sessions spanning days or weeks.
The Two Components
Component
What It Handles
Memory
Long-term knowledge retention across sessions (user preferences, project context, past interactions)
State
Short-term conversation state within a session (mutable messages, file cache, token usage, permission denials)
Session Memory (State)
The
QueryEngine
is the primary state container holding complete conversation history:
Key aspects of session state:
mutableMessages
— Every user message, assistant response, tool result, and system message appended incrementally
Cross-turn persistence
— State (messages, file cache, usage) carries over between
submitMessage()
calls
Mutable & incremental
— Messages added as conversation progresses, not reset between turns
Session Persistence via Transcripts
Conversations are
persisted to disk for recovery
Persistence Strategy
When Used
Trade-off
Fire-and-forget
Bare mode
Speed prioritized over durability
Block & flush
Interactive/cowork mode
Durability prioritized over speed
This enables
--resume
functionality
— without pre-emptive persistence, a kill between user message and API response makes resume fail with "No conversation found".
Memory Hierarchy (Long-Term)
Claude Code implements a
six-tier memory hierarchy
System prompt
— Agent's job description/identity
CLAUDE.md files
— Project-level and user-level instructions
Memory directory
— Persistent attachments attached automatically
Conversation history
— mutableMessages in QueryEngine
File state cache
— Cached file reads to avoid redundant reads
Transcripts
— Persisted conversation state on disk
Key behaviors:
CLAUDE.md paths tracked
loadedNestedMemoryPaths

set avoids re-injecting same file dozens of times
-
File cache evicts

— But CLAUDE.md files remain in memory directory
-
Preserved segments
— Ensure resume works correctly after compaction
Why Memory & State Matters
Without memory, each conversation starts from scratch — users must re-explain preferences, project context, and ongoing work every time.
Benefit
Explanation
Continuity
Agents build on past conversations, remember preferences, maintain context across sessions
Attribution
Memory provides attribution for learned information (where knowledge came from)
Self-correction
Agents can learn from past mistakes recorded in memory
State management
Cumulative token usage tracked to enforce budgets and warn before exceeding limits
Mental Model
Think of Memory & State like a
developer's workspace
State
= Open files, terminal history, current working directory (what's in front of them right now)
Memory
= Notes, documentation, project README, past decisions (what they've learned and stored)
Both are needed: state for immediate work, memory for long-term context.
Key Takeaways
Memory & State = state, persistence, and knowledge across sessions
QueryEngine is the primary state container holding complete conversation history (mutableMessages)
Every message (user, assistant, tool result, system) appended to mutableMessages incrementally
State persists across
submitMessage()
calls — enables multi-turn conversations
Transcripts persisted to disk for crash/restart recovery and
--resume
functionality
Claude Code implements six-tier memory hierarchy: system prompt → CLAUDE.md → memory dir → conversation → file cache → transcripts
Pre-emptive persistence ensures transcript is resumable even if killed before API responds
Memory provides attribution for learned information and turns errors into learning assets
The basic agentic building block is LLM + augmentations (retrieval, tools, memory)
Memory systems are fundamental to creating AI agents that feel continuous and coherent rather than amnesiac.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of memory & state helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with memory & state has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying memory & state in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Advanced Best Practices
Production experience with memory & state has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.

Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Advanced Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying memory & state in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Best Practices
Following best practices ensures effective use of Memory & State in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in Memory & State helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Performance and Optimization
Optimizing Memory & State for production environments requires attention to key performance factors.
Factor
Impact
Optimization Strategy
Latency
High
Use caching and streaming responses
Throughput
Medium
Implement request batching and connection pooling
Cost
High
Monitor token usage and optimize prompt length
Reliability
Critical
Add retries with exponential backoff
Security Considerations
Security is paramount when implementing Memory & State in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.
Summary

This section covered Memory & State in depth, including core concepts, practical implementation approaches, and production considerations. Apply these patterns consistently to build robust, maintainable systems.

attrs.alt: Agent Memory Hierarchy: Six Storage Tiers

content.text: Memory Types: A Taxonomy

Memory in AI agents is not a monolithic concept. Agents operate with multiple distinct types of memory that serve different purposes and have different characteristics in terms of persistence, retrievability, and cost. Understanding this taxonomy is essential for designing agents that maintain coherent state across the complexity of real-world tasks.

The core distinction is between in-context memory (anything visible to the model in the current API call) and out-of-context memory (information stored externally that must be explicitly retrieved). In-context memory is instantly accessible but limited by context window size and ephemeral across sessions. Out-of-context memory is persistent and unbounded but requires retrieval mechanisms.
Four Memory Categories
In-context (working memory):

The conversation history, tool results, and system prompt visible to the model in the current API call. Fastest to access, limited to the context window, lost when the session ends. This is what the model 'knows' right now.
External storage (long-term memory):
Files on disk, databases, vector stores, or key-value stores containing information the agent has written or retrieved. Persists across sessions. Must be explicitly retrieved using tool calls.
Semantic memory:
Knowledge about facts, concepts, and how things work. For Claude, this comes from training data (baked in) or from documents loaded into context. Supports answering 'what is X?' questions.
Episodic memory:

Records of specific events and interactions. Implemented via conversation transcripts, structured log files, or dedicated memory databases. Supports answering 'what happened when I worked on X?' questions.
Implementing External Memory with Vector Stores

For agents that need to remember large amounts of information across sessions, vector stores provide semantic retrieval — the ability to find relevant memories based on meaning rather than exact keyword match. This is how an agent can remember that 'the user prefers PostgreSQL for all new projects' when handling a database-related task, even if the user stated this preference weeks ago in different words.
